title: Type 3c (Pancreatogenic) Diabetes — Ewald & Bretzel Diagnostic Criteria
subtitle: Major vs minor criteria for pancreatogenic diabetes, fecal elastase-1 and pancreatic imaging requirements, exclusion of type 1 autoimmunity, and the limitations that keep type 3c underdiagnosed
authors: User-provided clinical summary citing Ewald & Bretzel diagnostic criteria for type 3c diabetes
sourceFile: user-provided content (chat); criteria attributed to Ewald and Bretzel (published type 3c diagnostic framework)

relatedChapters.title: Type 3c Diabetes with ABPA and Chronic Pancreatitis

items.title: Why Ewald and Bretzel proposed formal criteria for type 3c diabetes
items.content:
Pancreatogenic (type 3c) diabetes is estimated in 5–10% of all people with diabetes but is frequently misclassified as type 1 or type 2 because the pancreatic contribution is under-recognised. Ewald and Bretzel published the only widely cited diagnostic framework to separate true type 3c diabetes from other diabetes subtypes at a time when exocrine testing and pancreatic imaging had become clinically accessible.

The framework forces the clinician to document three linked facts before labelling type 3c: objective exocrine failure, structural pancreatic disease on imaging, and exclusion of autoimmune type 1 diabetes. Without this triad, 'diabetes plus abdominal pain' defaults to type 2 — and patients miss pancreatic enzyme replacement, fat-soluble-vitamin repletion, and insulin-centred management tailored to glucagon deficiency.
keyPoints: Type 3c is common but under-recognised — often mislabelled T1/T2
Ewald & Bretzel = only published formal diagnostic criteria
Triad links exocrine failure, imaging, and non-autoimmune diabetes
items.reference: Ewald & Bretzel Major Criteria: "In the only published criteria for type 3c diabetes, Ewald and Bretzel9 proposed the following major criteria (all must be present): exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (by monoclonal faecal elastase-1 testing or direct function tests), consistent pancreatic abnormalities on imaging (endoscopic ultrasound, MRI, or CT scan), and absence of related autoimmune markers of type 1 diabetes.9"

items.title: How to satisfy major criterion 1 — exocrine pancreatic insufficiency
items.content:
Major criterion 1 requires documented exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (EPI), preferably by monoclonal fecal elastase-1 (FE-1) or by direct pancreatic function testing (secretin stimulation, 72-hour fecal fat — rarely used in routine practice).

FE-1 is the practical first test: non-invasive, stable in stool, and widely available. In the type 3c context, EPI is nearly ubiquitous once diabetes from chronic pancreatitis is established — but clinically overt steatorrhea may be absent until >90% of exocrine function is lost, so absence of diarrhoea does not exclude EPI. A low FE-1 in a patient with pancreatic imaging abnormalities should trigger pancreatic enzyme replacement even when gastrointestinal symptoms are mild (see ht-002 for FE-1 interpretation bands).
keyPoints: EPI = monoclonal FE-1 or direct function tests
EPI common in type 3c even without steatorrhea
Low FE-1 + pancreatic disease → treat exocrine failure, not only glucose
items.reference: Ewald & Bretzel Major Criteria: "exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (by monoclonal faecal elastase-1 testing or direct function tests)"

items.title: How to satisfy major criterion 2 — pathological pancreatic imaging
items.content:
Major criterion 2 requires consistent pancreatic abnormalities on imaging — endoscopic ultrasound (EUS), MRI (including MRCP), or CT. The modality depends on local expertise: EUS is sensitive for early chronic pancreatitis and parenchymal calcification; MRI/MRCP maps ductal disease; CT detects calcification and atrophy.

Imaging proves that diabetes is arising in the setting of structural pancreatic disease — not incidental coexistence of type 2 diabetes with a normal gland. Normal pancreatic imaging makes type 3c unlikely unless another destructive process (post-pancreatectomy, haemochromatosis, tropical pancreatitis) is documented elsewhere in the record.
keyPoints: Acceptable modalities: EUS, MRI/MRCP, or CT
Imaging must show pathological pancreatic change
Normal gland imaging argues against type 3c
items.reference: Ewald & Bretzel Major Criteria: "consistent pancreatic abnormalities on imaging (endoscopic ultrasound, MRI, or CT scan)"

items.title: Why absence of type 1 diabetes autoimmune markers is a major criterion
items.content:
Major criterion 3 excludes autoimmune type 1 diabetes: islet autoantibodies (GAD-65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA where interpretable) should be absent. This prevents mislabelling autoimmune β-cell destruction as pancreatogenic diabetes when both can present in young lean patients.

A positive antibody panel redirects management toward insulin and autoimmune surveillance; antibody-negative status in the setting of EPI plus pancreatic imaging supports type 3c. Antibody testing is most informative near diagnosis — long-standing type 1 may become antibody-negative over time, which is one source of overlap with the Ewald & Bretzel framework.
keyPoints: Exclude T1DM autoimmunity — GAD, IA-2, ZnT8 (± IAA)
Antibody-positive → type 1 pathway, not type 3c
Antibody negativity most reliable near diabetes onset
items.reference: Ewald & Bretzel Major Criteria: "absence of related autoimmune markers of type 1 diabetes.9"

items.title: Why all three major criteria must be present together
items.content:
The Ewald & Bretzel framework requires all three major criteria simultaneously — not two of three. Each criterion closes a different false-positive route:

• EPI alone can accompany ageing, coeliac disease, or long-standing type 1/2 diabetes with pancreatic atrophy.
• Imaging abnormality alone may be chronic pancreatitis without diabetes, or an incidental finding with coexistent type 2 diabetes.
• Antibody-negative diabetes alone is MODY, type 2, or type 3c — non-specific.

Only the combination defines pancreatogenic diabetes as the primary classification. In practice, apply the triad when diabetes is first recognised in a patient with known or suspected pancreatic disease — not years later when overlap with other diabetes types has blurred the picture.
keyPoints: All three major criteria required — not two of three
Each criterion blocks a different misclassification
Best applied at diabetes presentation with pancreatic disease
items.reference: Ewald & Bretzel Major Criteria: "the following major criteria (all must be present)"

items.title: How the minor criteria strengthen — but do not replace — the major triad
items.content:
Minor criteria support the diagnosis when the major triad is met but are not sufficient alone:

• Impaired β-cell function — HOMA-B, C-peptide, or glucose concentrations
• Absence of excessive insulin resistance — HOMA-IR
• Impaired incretin secretion — low GLP-1 and/or pancreatic polypeptide (PP) response
• Low fat-soluble vitamins — A, D, E, K

The most clinically actionable minor signals are low C-peptide (insulin deficiency out of proportion to obesity), absent PP response to mixed-nutrient ingestion (discriminates type 3c from type 2), and low vitamin D despite supplementation (maldigestion from untreated EPI). Minor criteria are supportive evidence, not a standalone diagnostic gate.
keyPoints: Minor criteria: β-cell defect, low IR, impaired incretin/PP, low fat-soluble vitamins
PP response to feeding discriminates type 3c from type 2
Supportive only — cannot diagnose type 3c without major triad
items.reference: Minor Criteria: "Minor criteria included impaired β-cell function (as measured by homoeostatic model assessment for β-cell function, or C-peptide or glucose concentrations), absence of insulin resistance (as defined by homoeostatic model assessment for insulin resistance), impaired incretin secretion (glucagon-like peptide-1 [GLP-1] or pancreatic polypeptide, or both), and low serum concentrations of lipid soluble vitamins (A, D, E, and K)."

items.title: Why overlap with long-standing type 1 and type 2 diabetes weakens the criteria
items.content:
The criteria were designed for classification at diabetes presentation, but in real practice patients are often referred years later. Long-standing type 1 and type 2 diabetes both cause pancreatic atrophy and secondary exocrine insufficiency — so an older patient with type 2 for 20 years may have low FE-1 and imaging changes without true type 3c as the primary diagnosis.

Similarly, type 2 diabetes is common enough in the general population to coexist accidentally with chronic pancreatitis. The major triad may therefore be met in a patient whose hyperglycaemia is primarily type 2 with incidental pancreatic disease — the criteria cannot always separate 'diabetes caused by the pancreas' from 'diabetes plus pancreatic disease.' Clinical judgement and timing of onset remain essential.
keyPoints: Long-standing T1/T2 → pancreatic atrophy + secondary EPI
T2DM can coexist with chronic pancreatitis by chance
Criteria clearest at first presentation of diabetes
items.reference: Limitations of the Criteria: "overlap of features with long-standing type 1 and 2 diabetes (appendix)"

items.title: Why HOMA-based minor criteria are poorly standardised in type 3c
items.content:
Minor criteria invoke HOMA for β-cell function (HOMA-B) and insulin resistance (HOMA-IR), plus C-peptide and glucose — but these measures were validated primarily in type 2 diabetes populations. There is little standardisation of cutoffs for 'inadequate β-cell function' or 'absence of insulin resistance' in type 3c diabetes specifically, and high-quality published glucose-homeostasis data for type 3c remain limited.

Practically: use C-peptide with concurrent glucose (low C-peptide at raised glucose supports insulin deficiency) rather than relying on a single HOMA threshold. Do not exclude type 3c because HOMA-IR is elevated — coexistent insulin resistance is common in type 3c patients, which directly contradicts the minor criterion of 'absence of insulin resistance' as a hard rule.
keyPoints: HOMA-B/HOMA-IR lack type-3c-specific validated cutoffs
Sparse high-quality glucose-homeostasis data in type 3c
Coexistent insulin resistance is common — minor IR criterion is not absolute
items.reference: Limitations of the Criteria: "little standardisation in methods used to determine inadequate β-cell function and insulin resistance, absence of high-quality published data for glucose homoeostasis in type 3c diabetes, the presence of coexistent insulin resistance in many patients"

items.title: How to work up a patient when type 3c is suspected but criteria are incomplete
items.content:
Practical algorithm when pancreatogenic diabetes is suspected:

1. Document pancreatic disease history (chronic pancreatitis, pancreatectomy, haemochromatosis, tropical pancreatitis, cystic fibrosis, pancreatic cancer).
2. Order monoclonal fecal elastase-1 (solid stool sample; beware watery-stool dilution artefact — ht-002).
3. Image pancreas — EUS, MRI/MRCP, or CT as available.
4. Check islet autoantibody panel if age/onset suggests type 1 overlap.
5. Measure C-peptide with concurrent glucose; consider PP response to mixed meal in research/expert settings.
6. Check vitamins A, D, E, K — low levels support EPI and guide replacement.

If all three major criteria are met → classify type 3c and manage exocrine + endocrine together (insulin-centred glycaemic therapy, PERT, vitamin replacement — see cr-032). If criteria are incomplete but suspicion remains high, treat EPI empirically and classify as 'diabetes with pancreatic disease' while completing the work-up.
keyPoints: FE-1 + imaging + antibodies = core outpatient work-up
C-peptide/glucose and fat-soluble vitamins add support
Treat EPI while completing criteria if suspicion is high
items.reference: Ewald & Bretzel Major Criteria: "exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (by monoclonal faecal elastase-1 testing or direct function tests), consistent pancreatic abnormalities on imaging (endoscopic ultrasound, MRI, or CT scan), and absence of related autoimmune markers of type 1 diabetes.9"

items.title: Why resource availability limits real-world use of the criteria
items.content:
Full application of the Ewald & Bretzel framework assumes access to monoclonal FE-1 testing, high-quality pancreatic imaging (often EUS or MRI), islet autoantibody assays, and optionally dynamic tests of incretin and pancreatic polypeptide secretion. In resource-limited settings, clinicians may confirm only one or two elements — most often imaging plus clinical chronic pancreatitis — and still need to initiate PERT and insulin-based diabetes management without waiting for a perfect classification.

The criteria are a research and referral-centre standard, not an excuse to withhold enzyme replacement when FE-1 is low and pancreatic disease is documented. Under-treatment of EPI because 'type 3c is not formally confirmed' is a common and harmful pitfall.
keyPoints: Full criteria need FE-1, imaging, antibodies, sometimes dynamic tests
Resource limits delay formal classification
Do not withhold PERT when EPI + pancreatic disease are documented
items.reference: Limitations of the Criteria: "the availability of adequate radiological and laboratory resources."

items.question: According to the Ewald and Bretzel criteria for type 3c diabetes, which combination is required for diagnosis?
options: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency, pathological pancreatic imaging, and absence of type 1 diabetes autoantibodies
Low C-peptide, elevated HOMA-IR, and positive GAD antibody
Fecal elastase-1 below 200 µg/g alone
Chronic pancreatitis history and HbA1c above 6.5% without imaging
items.explanation: All three major criteria must be present: EPI (FE-1 or direct function tests), consistent pancreatic imaging abnormality (EUS, MRI, or CT), and absence of type 1 autoimmune markers. Minor criteria and clinical history alone are insufficient.
items.reference: Ewald & Bretzel Major Criteria: "the following major criteria (all must be present): exocrine pancreatic insufficiency... consistent pancreatic abnormalities on imaging... and absence of related autoimmune markers of type 1 diabetes.9"

items.question: A 48-year-old man with chronic pancreatitis has diabetes, fecal elastase-1 65 µg/g, and CT showing pancreatic calcification. GAD-65 antibody is negative. How should his diabetes be classified under Ewald & Bretzel criteria?
options: Type 3c (pancreatogenic) diabetes — all three major criteria are met
Type 2 diabetes — antibody-negative diabetes is always type 2
Type 1 diabetes — diabetes plus pancreatic disease equals autoimmune disease
Cannot classify until HOMA-IR is below 2.5
items.explanation: Low FE-1 documents EPI, CT shows pathological pancreatic change, and negative GAD-65 satisfies the autoimmune exclusion. All three major criteria are met → type 3c. HOMA-IR is a minor supportive criterion, not a major gate, and coexistent insulin resistance does not exclude type 3c.
items.reference: Ewald & Bretzel Major Criteria: "the following major criteria (all must be present): exocrine pancreatic insufficiency... consistent pancreatic abnormalities on imaging... and absence of related autoimmune markers of type 1 diabetes.9"

items.question: Which finding is a minor (supportive) — not major — criterion in the Ewald & Bretzel type 3c framework?
options: Low serum vitamin D in the setting of documented EPI
Monoclonal fecal elastase-1 confirming exocrine insufficiency
MRI showing chronic pancreatitis with ductal dilatation
Negative IA-2 and ZnT8 antibodies
items.explanation: Low fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are minor criteria. FE-1, imaging abnormalities, and absence of T1DM antibodies are the three major criteria.
items.reference: Minor Criteria: "low serum concentrations of lipid soluble vitamins (A, D, E, and K)."

items.question: A 62-year-old woman has had type 2 diabetes for 18 years. New fecal elastase-1 is 140 µg/g and CT shows mild pancreatic atrophy. She is obese with HOMA-IR 4.2. What is the main limitation in applying Ewald & Bretzel criteria here?
options: Long-standing type 2 diabetes can cause secondary EPI and atrophy — overlap makes primary type 3c classification uncertain
Obesity excludes all forms of diabetes except type 2 by definition
Fecal elastase-1 cannot be measured after age 60
HOMA-IR above 2.0 confirms type 2 and rules out pancreatic disease
items.explanation: Long-standing type 1 and type 2 diabetes overlap with features of type 3c (pancreatic atrophy, secondary EPI). Coexistent insulin resistance is also common and expected. The criteria are most reliable at diabetes presentation, not decades into type 2 disease.
items.reference: Limitations of the Criteria: "overlap of features with long-standing type 1 and 2 diabetes" and "the presence of coexistent insulin resistance in many patients"

items.question: A patient meets all three Ewald & Bretzel major criteria for type 3c diabetes. Which management principle follows directly from the exocrine criterion?
options: Start pancreatic enzyme replacement and assess fat-soluble vitamins even if steatorrhea is absent
Begin sulfonylurea therapy as first-line because C-peptide may be preserved
Avoid insulin because glucagon counter-regulation is intact in type 3c
Defer fecal elastase testing once HbA1c is at target
items.explanation: Documented EPI mandates PERT and fat-soluble-vitamin assessment regardless of overt steatorrhea — clinically significant maldigestion may be silent until exocrine function is severely reduced. Type 3c is insulin- and glucagon-deficient (sulfonylureas hazardous; insulin is mainstay) — see cr-032.
items.reference: Ewald & Bretzel Major Criteria: "exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (by monoclonal faecal elastase-1 testing or direct function tests)"

items.statement: Under Ewald & Bretzel criteria, two of three major criteria are sufficient to diagnose type 3c diabetes if minor criteria are also present.
items.explanation: False. All three major criteria must be present. Minor criteria support but cannot substitute for missing major criteria.
items.reference: Ewald & Bretzel Major Criteria: "the following major criteria (all must be present)"

items.statement: The Ewald & Bretzel criteria acknowledge that coexistent insulin resistance is common in type 3c diabetes, limiting the utility of HOMA-IR as a minor criterion.
items.explanation: True. The framework notes coexistent insulin resistance in many patients and poor standardisation of HOMA-based measures in type 3c — so 'absence of insulin resistance' cannot be applied as an absolute minor criterion in practice.
items.reference: Limitations of the Criteria: "little standardisation in methods used to determine inadequate β-cell function and insulin resistance" and "the presence of coexistent insulin resistance in many patients"

items.statement: A normal pancreatic CT scan alone can exclude type 3c diabetes in a patient with diabetes and low fecal elastase-1.
items.explanation: False. Major criterion 2 requires pathological pancreatic imaging — normal imaging makes type 3c unlikely by this framework. But low FE-1 with diabetes still warrants full imaging (EUS may detect disease missed on CT) and exocrine treatment; the criteria emphasise imaging abnormality as part of the triad, not FE-1 alone.
items.reference: Ewald & Bretzel Major Criteria: "consistent pancreatic abnormalities on imaging (endoscopic ultrasound, MRI, or CT scan)"

items.assertion: Absence of type 1 diabetes autoimmune markers is a major criterion in the Ewald & Bretzel type 3c framework.
items.reason: Autoimmune type 1 diabetes and pancreatogenic diabetes can both present as insulin-deficient diabetes in non-obese patients, and the distinction changes long-term management.
items.explanation: Both true; excluding T1DM autoimmunity is a major criterion precisely because the phenotypes overlap and management diverges (autoimmune surveillance vs pancreatic enzyme replacement and pancreatogenic insulin strategy).
items.reference: Ewald & Bretzel Major Criteria: "absence of related autoimmune markers of type 1 diabetes.9"

items.assertion: The Ewald & Bretzel criteria are most reliably applied at the initial presentation of diabetes rather than decades into established type 2 diabetes.
items.reason: Long-standing type 1 and type 2 diabetes can produce pancreatic atrophy and secondary exocrine insufficiency that overlap with type 3c features.
items.explanation: Both true; overlap with long-standing T1/T2 is a stated limitation, so timing of application matters — early classification is more specific.
items.reference: Limitations of the Criteria: "overlap of features with long-standing type 1 and 2 diabetes (appendix)"
